title: Babylon by Bus
author: Donovan Webster & Ray Lemoine
note: An incredible true story, but one whose second half turned out to be a yawn, bogged down by a narrator infatuated with himself.

title: Ballistics
author: Billy Collins
note: Do yourself a favor, put down this letter, go over to your computer, type in Amazon and find this book, order it. This is the most accessible book of poetry published in the last ten years.

title: Blue Like Jazz
author: Donald Miller
note: A well written, thoughtful, frank and honest story about one man's journey for meaning in his life. Buy this one and read it this year!

title: Love and Respect
author: Dr. Emerson Eggerichs
note: This book should be mandatory reading for all married or soon to be married couples. From a purely analytical aspect, it hits at the heart of the issue most important to men and women.

title: My Sister's Keeper
author: Jodi Picoult
note: A tear-jerker to be sure, but a little too convenient in the ending (although Emily disagrees with my own all too convenient one line synopsis of the book).

title: The Hakawati
author: Rabig Alemeddine
note: A very long book (over 500 pages) that is really a loosely connected conglomeration of old Persian folk tales. This book took us months to read.

title: The Memory Keeper's Daughter
author: Kim Edwards
note: While heart wrenching, this novel about the dangers of buried secrets fails when the author refuses to take a stand on any of the characters' flawed decisions.

title: The Shack
author: W.M. Paul Young
note: Yes, this was on all the best-seller lists, but most importantly this is the first book that Emily and I ever read together. Beautiful imagery and relatable metaphors peripherally address the issue of why bad things happen. I say peripherally because in reading the book you realize that you will never get an answer to this question because it's the wrong question to ask in the first place.

title: The Time Traveler's Wife
author: Audrey Niffenegger
note: A heartbreaking story that addresses both the complexities and strength of love. Time travel hasn't been this interesting since Michael J. Fox hopped in the Delorean!

title: Three Cups of Tea
author: Greg Mortenson
note: An amazing tale of perseverance and sacrifice, but we couldn't help but feel bad for his lonely wife and kids who seem to play second fiddle to his quest to help the children and the people of Afghanistan. UPDATE: This guy has since proved a fraud in a pretty major way.

title: Water for Elephants
author: Sara Gruen
note: If you ever wondered what circus life was like during the depression era, check out this quick read. And what a great movie this will be!

title: A Million Miles in a Thousand Years
author: Donald Miller

title: Matterhorn
author: Karl Marlantes
summary: Matterhorn is a novel about Vietnam. More succinctly, it's THE best novel about Vietnam and ONE of the BEST on war ever written. Marlantes was a Yale grad and a Rhodes scholar who served IN Vietnam, was awarded the Navy Cross, the Bronze Star, two Navy Commendation Medals for valor, two Purple Hearts, and ten air medals. And for the past 30 years he has been working on this soul-wrenching story. We spend a disproportionate amount of time studying the facts, tactics, techniques and history of wars. I add this novel to my 'must read for FAOs' list because it adds something missing: CONTEXT and TEXTURE. The words 'acceptable loss' and 'collateral damage' no longer remain as easily typed black words on an after action report, instead they appear in the murky purple of blood and hopelessness.
quotes: We spend a disproportionate amount of time studying the facts, tactics, techniques and history of wars. I add this novel to my 'must read for FAOs' list because it adds something missing: CONTEXT and TEXTURE.

title: It Happened on the Way to War
author: Rye Barcott
note: This book is the embodiment of compassion and empathy.
quote: Talent is universal, opportunity is not.

title: The Hunger Games Trilogy
author: Suzanne Collins
note: The official Team Jamily book recommendation for 2011!

title: The Zanzibar Chest
author: Aidan Hartley
summary: From time to time, God causes men to be born who have a lust to go abroad at the risk of their lives and discover news. If there were ever an engraving to be mounted on the wall of my hypothetical office it would be Kipling's opening quote from this book. One of my favorite books ever — not technically fiction but it sure reads like it. Hartley was a war correspondent who covered Africa from Somalia to Rwanda to the Congo in the most brutal years. My complete notes and highlights are on FUUO.
quotes: From time to time, God causes men to be born who have a lust to go abroad at the risk of their lives and discover news.

title: Things Fall Apart
author: Chinua Achebe
quote: It is more difficult and more bitter when a man fails alone.

title: Americanah
author: Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie
summary: A riotous look at the fictional-ish journey of one Nigerian woman to the college campuses and social circles of the US. Tuck a copy of Adichie's deft and funny tale into your bag to read this summer a la plage ou a la piscine. Among her many insights into our culture is the American propensity to give visitors a full tour of their home — in Nigeria you will never get past the kitchen and living room.
quotes: You must nod back when a black person nods at you in a heavily white area. It is called the black nod. It is a way for black people to say "You are not alone, I am here too."
How was it possible to miss something you no longer wanted?

title: Billy Lynn's Long Halftime Walk
author: Ben Fountain
note: An insightful and side-splitting fictional look at the American obsession with "supporting the troops."

title: Crossroads
author: W.M. Paul Young
note: Thought-provoking but not as good as The Shack.

title: Disgrace
author: J.M. Coetzee
note: One of South Africa's premier writers of our time. This story is about pride, lust, and the rural aftermath of a post-apartheid South Africa.

title: Ender's Game
author: Oroson Scott Card
summary: A million times better than the mediocre movie. Start with this one and revel in reading the whole series.
quotes: In the moment when I truly understand my enemy, understand him well enough to defeat him, then in that very moment I also love him. I think it's impossible to really understand somebody, what they want, what they believe, and not love them the way they love themselves. And then, in that very moment when I love them...I destroy them.

title: Foe
author: J.M. Coetzee
note: Wow. What a weird book. Coetzee reimagines the Robinson Crusoe story. I know it means something deeper and if I was getting graded I could figure it out.

title: Long Walk to Freedom
author: Nelson Mandela
summary: I started Mandela's autobiography a few weeks before his passing after watching it gather digital dust on my kindle bookshelf for over a year. My reticence to dive into this book stemmed from a general disdain for dense, bloated, self-important celebrations of one's life often captured in autobiographies. My misgivings could not have been more misplaced. More a series of chronological vignettes than a traditional biography, Madiba beautiful and humbly tells not only his story — but that of South Africa.
quotes: To be free is not merely to cast off one's chains, but to live in a way that respects and enhances the freedom of others. The true test of our devotion to freedom is just beginning.
It is said that no one truly knows a nation until one has been inside its jails. A nation should not be judged by how it treats its highest citizens, but its lowest ones.
To make peace with an enemy one must work with that enemy, and that enemy becomes one's partner.

title: Never Let Me Go
author: Kazuo Ishiguro
note: Ishiguro has an ear for the naivete of youthful aspiration and a heart for tragedy--a great story.

title: Orphan's Master Son
author: Adam Jonnson
note: This incredible story falls firmly into the category fiction-ish. This lengthy tome is part spy caper, swashbuckling romance, scathing indictment, political commentary, but is fully engaging. Johnson imagines the narrative arc of one ordinary North Korean citizen who struggles for an identity amidst a government-imposed monolithic culture. This arc spans the globe from North Korea to Japan to Texas--you won't be disappointed.

title: People Who Eat Darkness
author: Richard Lloyd Parry
note: A pretty creepy (true) story that examines a seedy sub-section of Japanese culture. Incredibly well-written but I wouldn't recommend it for your small town book club.

title: Prodigal God
author: Tim Keller
summary: First off, as an English major I am embarrassed to admit that I did not know the correct definition of the word prodigal. I always thought it referred to someone who screws up and comes back around, hat in hand. I couldn't be further from the truth — prodigal means someone who is wastefully extravagant, who spends their resources recklessly and freely. So in this story what you really have is a parable about two lost sons and a reckless (with his love) father. Keller's reorientation of the story from its traditional focus on the wayward son gives one more accurate insight into just who this tale was for. In context, Jesus' main audience was the 'older brothers' — the self-righteous pharisees who sought to control their own salvation through rigorous adherence to the law. One of best parts of Prodigal God is that it turns the reader outward showing that recognition of God's costly grace should spur his followers to serve the poor, to love the orphans and widows in society today. On my yearly re-reading list.
quotes: Prodigal means someone who is wastefully extravagant, who spends their resources recklessly and freely.
The lavish and extravagant call of the Father to sons and daughters of all types—to join him in the banquet.

title: The Light Between Oceans
author: M.L. Stedman
note: This one is a tear-jerker--read it in the privacy of your living room.

title: Warriors: Life and Death Among the Somalis
author: Gerald Hanley
note: Read everything that you can get your hands on by this gifted author. As Africa threw off the chains of colonial oppression, Hanley examines the Somali experience through the eyes of a British soldier stationed there.

title: West with the Night
author: Beryl Markham
summary: An aviation pioneer—the first woman to fly from Europe to North America solo—an accomplished horse trainer—illicit lover of the Duke of Gloucester—writer without equal. As a former pilot myself, I am astounded and in awe of the pioneering pilots of the 1920s and 30s in Africa. West with the Night is one of the best written books ever. Hemingway's evaluation of Beryl in a letter to a friend should rocket this novel to the top of your amazon wish list: 'She can write rings around all of us who consider ourselves as writers.'
quotes: You can live a lifetime and, at the end of it, know more about other people than you know about yourself.

title: A Hologram for the King
author: Dave Eggers
note: Reading Eggers is easy. This is a credit to the skill and imagination of the author. In Hologram we get a glimpse into the bizarre alternate reality of the middle east and its ridiculous wealth.

title: A Sport and a Pastime
author: James Salter
summary: Salter's most celebrated novel and the one that cemented his reputation as a writer's writer. Set in France, it captures light, place, and desire with a precision that makes most other novelists look like they're working in watercolors while Salter works in oil. His prose is the standard by which I measure every other novelist.
quotes: Write as if this were your only book, your last book. Into it put everything you were saving—everything precious, every scrap of capital. Don't be afraid of being left with nothing.

title: Country of My Skull: Guilt, Sorrow, and the Limits of Forgiveness in the New South Africa
author: Antjie Krog
note: So does Country of My Skull, however, I found it a little too dense and never made it through the whole thing.

title: Empire of the Summer Moon: Quanah Parker and the Rise and Fall of the Comanches, the Most Powerful Indian Tribe in American History
author: S.C. Gwynne
note: This led book led me to Empire of the Summer Moon, a masterful recounting of the rise and fall of the Comanche indians in the United States.

title: Every Day is for the Thief
author: Teju Cole
note: I felt cheated reading Cole's Every Day is for the Thief. I felt like it needed to be one-third longer. The story of a Nigerian returning home after living in New York City reads both as a love story to Lagos as well as the gossiping critique of a disappointed lover.

title: Golden Hour
author: Todd Moss
note: I liked The Golden Hour. Moss is an economist by trade and it took guts craft this story of an African coups and the white men who try to save the country.

title: Jesus > Religion: Why He Is So Much Better Than Trying Harder, Doing More, and Being Good Enough
author: Jefferson Bethke
note: Jesus>Religion is a book by the "I Hate Religion Guy". The book is a more nuanced explanation. Whether you believe or not, this book is a worthwhile read--to at least challenge your perceptions of the Christian community and church.

title: Lean Life
author: Brooks Hollan
note: I will admit that I was skeptical as I began Hollan's hard to categorize fitness story/manual/self-help book. Initially it felt a little gimmicky to dispense fitness advice through a fictional story. Soon, however, I changed my tune as I realized that his methodology in doing so is actually perfect because the principles he recommends stick with you because you remember little Timmy's story. BZ Brooks.

title: Marriage in Domoni
author: Martin Ottenheimer
note: You can skip the Marriage in Domoni tome unless you are planning to live/visit Comoros. I enjoyed it from an academic perspective but don't expect a breezy read.

title: My Traitor's Heart
author: Rian Malan
note: Of these 6 books, I would only truly recommend Long Walk to Freedom, My Traitor's Heart and Bring Me My Machine Gun. The latter two are nice companions to Mandela's autobiography and they bring the reader through the details of the post-apartheid era.

title: Say Her Name
author: Francisco Goldman
note: Say Her Name is the sorrowful yet beautiful true story of the author's wife's unfortunate death and ode to remembrance and the its aftermath.

title: Strong Fathers Strong Daughters
author: Meg Meeker
note: Strong Fathers, Strong Daughters is on my yearly RE-reading list for good reason. You want find better advice on how to love, support, discipline and bond with your daughter through the spectrum of her life.

title: The Battle for the Soul of South Africa, from Mandela to Zuma
author: Alec Russel

title: The Fault in our Stars
author: John Green
note: The Fault in our Stars also deals with the emotional juxtaposition of the impending approach of death and the deep diving soaring sensation of young love.

title: The Hunters
author: James Salter
note: Start with The Hunters, a quasi-autobiographical tale of an aging Korean War ace and then pick any of his others--they won't disappoint.

title: The Love Affairs of Nathaniel P: A Novel
author: Adelle Waldman
note: The Love Affairs is a biting critique on the millennial hipster-esque predisposition for a Potemkin's village version of love in Brooklyn. The author has a keen eye for the inner monologue of most 20 something's navigating the dating pool today.

title: The Son
author: Phillip Meyer
note: The Son is one of the stories that plunges you into a portion of US history that you likely only encountered as a page in your history books during high school. In the book, the life of a Texas patriarch is recounted through two centuries beginning with an adolescent's capture by a band of Comanche indians.

title: Unbroken: A World War II Story of Survival, Resilience, and Redemption
author: Laura Hillenbrand
note: I can't say enough about Unbroken. If you haven't read it yet I am actually jealous because it's that good. If you've seen the movie you should still read the book as the movie does not do justice to the incredible life of this man (and it leaves out the latter third of his life).

title: War
author: Sebastian Junger
note: Sebastian Junger's eye for detail is equal to his hunger for authenticity in his writing. Junger's book is not about Afghanistan or the Taliban--these are merely the backdrop for his penetrating examination of the men who go to war. In dividing WAR into three books--fear, killing and love--Junger lays out his hypothesis that these three emotions (or actions) encompass war for the young men of the United States.

title: White Fire
author: Douglas Preston & Lincoln Child
note: White Fire is a classic thriller cum mystery in the vein of Sherlock Holmes. It's great to pair with the terrifying tale Gone Girl.

title: Wool
author: Hugh Howey
note: Howey began his writing career self-publishing. So you should start with his breakout hit Wool and go from there.

title: 7 Women and the Secret to their Greaness
author: Eric Metaxas
note: Read it because you will be floored by the stories you didn't know--like a beer-swilling, cigarette-smoking saint and nun. Metaxas is in his element when it comes to biographies, particularly these briefer volumes, versus his voluminous accounts of Bonhoeffer and Wilberforce.

title: All the Names
author: Jose Saramago
note: Saramago is a Nobel Prize winner, so if I wasn't crazy about his book something must be wrong with me, I guess. Ultimately, All the Names is a story about identity and the significance of remembering, following a beaten-down aging clerk at a Department of Births and Deaths in a Portuguese town who discovers the card of a woman and sets out to find out who she is. For me, though, this novel lacked action--the book turns out to be a character study of the clerk. So if you love a good character study, this is your book.

title: Amazing Grace: William Wilberforce and the Heroic Campaign to End Slavery
author: Eric Metaxas
note: Metaxas' essential tome on the life of William Wilberforce is not an easy read. His categoric description of Wilberforce's life is thick with minute details and a sometimes overwrought narrative that can make it a challenge to wade through its pages. This is not to take away from the skill of Metaxas--my reading tastes just happen to run more Hemingway than Faulkner. Regardless, I don't know many authors out there today with the researching acumen of Metaxas--one has only to read his untouchable Bonhoeffer to be assured of this.

title: Better: A Surgeon's Note on Performance
author: Atul Gawande
note: We just finished listening to this book. Unfortunately listening to a book over a period of six months during your daily commute doesn't lend itself to great notes, but we will just say that this book is mandatory reading for every health care professional...or anyone who steps foot in a hospital. It will make you MUCH more observant about making sure that everyone you come into contact with in a hospital has washed and disinfected their hands.

title: Burning the Days
author: James Salter
summary: Salter's memoir is the book that made me understand what writing could be. His career arc from West Point fighter pilot to revered literary novelist is unlike anything else in American letters. The prose burns throughout — there is not a wasted sentence. Reading this sent me back to read everything else he ever wrote.
quotes: The poets, writers, the sages and voices of their time, they are a chorus, the anthem they share is the same: the great and the small are joined, the beautiful lives, the other dies, and all is foolish except honor, love, and what little is known by the heart.

title: City of Lies
author: Ramita Navai
note: Read it because you will learn about an Iran that you never knew existed, and because it is dangerous and ignorant to fetishize the depravity of any enemy without taking the time to learn about its people and culture. Navai pens an expansive love song to the city of Tehran, recreating eight character studies representing different segments of Iranian culture.

title: Confessions of a CPA: The Truth About Life Insurance
author: Bryan S Bloom
note: Read it because it will expand your knowledge of the art of the possible when it comes to saving money. My main critique of this book is the incredibly poor editing job--even basic proof-reading would have caught several of the mistakes.

title: Confines of the Shadow
author: Alessandro Spina
note: Read it because you want to understand European colonialism in a deeper context, or because you love beautiful, witty writing in historical fiction. Alessandro Spina was a Syrian Maronite who grew up in Benghazi, was educated and wrote in Italian, and over 40 years penned an extraordinary cycle of novels about the establishment, flourishing and troubled aftermath of the Italian colony in Libya.

title: Everything is Yours, Everything is Not Yours
author: Clemantine Wamariya,

title: Fearless: The Undaunted Courage and Ultimate Sacrifice of Navy SEAL Team SIX Operator
author: Adam Brown
note: Fearless is one of the most incredible life stories that I ever heard. And happiest. And inspiring. And most tragic. And saddest. And unforgettable. I don't care who you are or what you believe. Just pick up this book and read about a man named Adam Brown--his story will stick with you, and might even make you a better husband, father and/or Christian. I read this book in three days.

title: Halfway Home
author: Hugh Howey
note: You'll see there aren't a lot of highlights from Halfway Home--look, you aren't reading Howey for lyrical lines that you'll tuck away your whole life. But I look forward to new books by Howey almost more than any other author. A master planet sets out to colonize the universe--until the kill sequence meant to cull uninhabitable landings is interrupted and a group survives.

title: Humilitas: A Lost Key to Life, Love, and Leadership
author: John Dickson
note: In Humilitas Dickson takes a deep academic look at the history of humility and its shift from an ancient term of denigration to an aspirational one. Most usefully, he links and develops the ways in which humility can make one a more effective leader. Unfortunately today humility is all too often an ignored virtue--Dickson, however, does an admirable job in bringing it back to the cultural forefront.

title: Light Years
author: James Salter
quote: You're so American. You believe everything is possible, everything will come.

title: One Day I Will Write About This Place: A Memoir
author: Binyavanga Wainaina
note: I almost gave up on One Day I Will Write About This Place because I wasn't prepared for the author's writing style--which isn't really a style at all--it is his way of capturing and describing the thoughts of a dreaming, introspective young boy growing up in Kenya.

title: Redeployment
author: Phil Klay
note: With less than .4% of Americans serving in the military, this is the book the other 99.6% should read. The idea of coming home as another deployment aptly captures what it's like for so many of our troops who fight our wars and then are supposed to just come back to a place that doesn't feel like home anymore. Ultimately, reading this book serves as a cathartic communion of sorts for the reader, a chance for those who haven't served to break bread with those who have sacrificed so much.
quote: Getting back feels like your first breath after nearly drowning. Even if it hurts, it's good.

title: Seven Men and the Secret of Their Greatness
author: Eric Metaxas
note: Metaxas hits his literary stride in 7 Men, offering a very readable and concise portrait of seven key figures in the Christian faith and the global stage. 7 Men contains echoes of all these elements as the reader meets Jackie Robinson, Chuck Colson, William Wilberforce, Bonhoeffer, George Washington, Eric Liddell and Pope John Paul II.

title: Shell Collector
author: Anthony Doerr
note: Read it because it's the same reason you read all of Howey's books--they are thrilling, speedy reads with a driving narrative that grips you from the first few pages. That said, this isn't his best, but I applaud his efforts branching out of his comfort zone into what might be categorized as eco-thriller.

title: The Cost of Living Like This
author: James Kennaway
note: One of my favorite things is to have a writer I love tell me about writers he loves. Having just discovered writers' writer Salter a year ago, I was happy to go down the rabbit hole and find out who this writer was that Salter thought worth mentioning in his own biography. I was impressed by Kennaway's story of one man's gradual descent into destruction, but what struck me most about Living Like This was the author's descriptive abilities.

title: The Fishermen
author: Chigozie Obioma
note: This is Obioma's first novel, and it has been met with widespread critical acclaim. Obioma stands as the heir apparent to Achebe because of his writing acumen in translating and capturing complex political events with parables and fables. In Fishermen, we hear the tale of a family falling apart amidst the backdrop of the turbulent 1993 Nigerian elections.

title: The Martian
author: Andy Weir
note: I will be the first to admit--I didn't understand even half of all the technical jargon that Andy Weir throws down in The Martian. But Weir can write--I tore through this tale of intergalactic ingenuity and relentlessness in three days. Thrilling, yet lined with humor, this is a perfect read for a plane ride or road trip.

title: War of the Encyclopaedists
author: Christopher Robinson and Gavin Kovite
note: Kovite and Robinson have written something special together--they've penned a story that is gripping and unique, funny and sad, a book that's hard to put down. War of the Encyclopaedists is a story about war, love and friendship, and the authors are part of a growing crop of writers setting the Iraq war into a context the greater American culture can digest.

title: We Need New Names
author: Noviolet Bulawayo
note: Bulawayo tells a masterful tale of a childhood in a crumbling country and an escape to life in America. In the first half of the novel she recounts, with an amazing ear for dialect and inflection, a life growing up on the streets as she and her band of friends roamed their city. The detail and richness of her tale reminded me that everyone has a story. In the end, New Names is a heartbreaking story about home, and childhood, not always being somewhere you can go back to.

title: A Bend in the River
author: V.S. Naipaul
note: This is one of those novels that immerses you into the life of a person and place--in this case an Indian Muslim transplant named Salim who wanders from his 'home' on the east coast of Africa to the DRC river town of Kisangani during the colonial independence era. As the week passed and I waded deeper into this tale, I found myself thinking about the characters during idle moments. It's one of those rare books that spreads its tentacles around you, the reader, and refuses to release you.

title: A Grief Observed
author: C.S. Lewis
note: Lewis recounts an honest and open account of one man's reckoning with the loss of his wife--tragically he only had a few short years with her before she died after a battle with cancer. This is one of those books that you hope you never have to be glad you read. What makes this short book special is Lewis' honesty, as his wife's death causes him to question everything that he believes, including his own faith. Ultimately, Lewis emerges from his sorrow with a deepened faith.

title: All Our Names
author: Dinaw Mengestu
summary: Read It Because it will Break Your Heart. And it's the Great African Novel. As I read through the final pages of the novel and watched the final paragraphs fade away my heart began to ache and break because I didn't want to let the characters go. And then I focused and slowly read through the final line. I won't ruin it for you but that line — oh that line — it will break your heart.
quotes: No one needs to learn how to kill, but it took the foreigners who came to Africa to show us that it meant nothing to do so.

title: Allah is Not Obliged
author: Ahmadou Kourouma
note: Well, it's hard to say that you 'love' a book about child soldiers, but Kourouma has crafted a first person narrative that is brutal and funny. Allah is Not Obliged is the tale of a young Ivorian boy named Birahima who becomes a child soldier of misfortune, killing and pontificating his way across West Africa. The best portions of the novel are the quasi histories of the different countries--Kourouma boils down the essential tenets of the political and warfield battles in an easy to read and digestible manner.

title: An African in Greenland
author: Tété-Michel Kpomassie
note: It was unlike anything I've ever read. This is the fantastical post-independence autobiographical tale of a young Togolese boy that leaves his 25 siblings on a whim (basically) and starts a 12-year journey from his native country to the chilly northern reaches of Greenland. It all started after he saw a book on Greenland in a missionary book store in his village.

title: Ghana Must Go
author: Taiye Selasi
note: This is not a novel about Africa, or Ghana, or Nigeria or immigrants (only), this is one of the classic novels about family, identity, loss, and forgiveness that follows you around in your idle thoughts long after you are finished. Selasi's mastery is most evident as she captures the sights, sounds and people of Ghana and Nigeria. The element that makes this such a timeless story is that it's ultimately about the power and indefatigability of a mother's love and hope.

title: Houseboy
author: Ferdinand Oyono
note: Oyono crafts a beautiful, tragic and damning tale about being a "black Frenchman" in Cameroon. This question of identity is at the center of this tale of a houseboy named Toundi with hopes of assimilation and upward mobility. With his near-to-last dying words being the question, "What are we blackmen who are called French?", one gets the sense that it's not going to end well for the young man.

title: I Did Not Come to You by Chance
author: Adaobi Tricia Nwaubani
note: You'll finally get to know that Nigerian prince who has been emailing you! In her debut novel Nwaubani follows in the footsteps of Adichie as she pulls back the curtain on the 419 industry in Nigeria--those oddly written emails from a Nigerian prince or businessman who needs your help to access a windfall. With an eye for humor and for heart, Nwaubani crafts a classic tragedy that follows the rise of Kingsley as he ascends a 419 crime syndicate, humanizing him as he becomes more and more adept at swindling gullible mugus.

title: In Praise of Savagery
author: Warwick Cairns
note: You will learn about the incredible journeys that the original European adventurers made as they explored the previously "undiscovered" interior of Africa. In Praise of Savagery is the memoir of a young man who befriended the great British explorer Wilfred Thesiger. Eventually, Cairns gets the opportunity to essentially retrace Thesiger's original steps when he first set out to find the origin of the Awash River in Ethiopia.

title: Leaving Tangier
author: Tahar Ben Jelloun
note: This was a sad, tragic, beautifully written tale about loneliness, despair, hope and home--in other words, it encompasses the entire range of the human condition that you expect a great novel to cover. Leaving Tangier stands the test of time as I read it ten years after its publication. It's a timely tale about the harsh reality of immigration and emigration and the depths that desperation drives one to.

title: Parenting with Love and Logics
author: Charles Fay and Foster Clin
note: You want to come up with a systematic approach to raise your kids into adults with healthy self-esteem who have the skills to make wise decisions for themselves. The authors divide your conflict interactions with kids into two broad categories: ones that affect you in some way, and those that affect your kids only--the latter requires a more deft touch where you allow your kids to experience the consequences of a poor decision, WITH EMPATHY.

title: Salvage the Bones
author: Jesmyn Ward
note: You will be reminded of both the devastating scope and power of Hurricane Katrina but also of the largely forgotten swathes of the American population. Jesmyn Ward creates a world and captures its dialogue and cultural inner monologue with a level of detail and tenderness that will keep its characters alive in your thoughts long after you've finished the novel. Ward's artistry is most strikingly displayed in how she somehow manages to humanize a character that dogfights his nursing pitbull.

title: Ships of Mercy
author: Don Stephens
note: You will see the transformation of a dream into the reality of millions of lives changed forever. Don Stephens' story is an incredible one--that Mercy Ships is the successful organization that it is today is nothing short of a miracle. A man had a dream to one day have a fleet of hospital ships that provide free surgeries to the poorest nations in the world. Today Mercy Ships has already served millions and is set to serve even more.

title: So Long a Letter
author: Mariama Bâ
note: Read it because you get to peer into the birth of feminism for a Muslim woman in post-independence Senegal. It took me a little bit to get into this story, mainly because I'd never read a book whose format was a simple, lengthy meandering letter. Ultimately, Ba won me over with her tender, thoughtful introspective look at the life of a cuckolded, then sister-wifed, then widowed, then independent and finally (maybe) free woman.

title: Stories
author: John Armstrong
note: Unfortunately, Armstrong has chosen not to try to publish his striking memoir of short vignettes that span the course of his memorable life. The depth of this level of emotional honesty laid bare on the pages are unlike anything I've ever read.

title: Superforecasting
author: Philip E. Tetlock and Dan M. Gardner

title: The Girl on the Train
author: Paula Hawkins
note: You can finish it in three days (or in one day at the beach), it's that fast of a read. Aside from being a pretty dark novel this is a perfect beach read. This story touches upon all the major nerves: falling in love, betrayal, jealousy, depression, murder, delusion--you get it all. Hawkins does a great job masking the outcome from the reader--this is the pull that keeps the pages turning.

title: The Jive Talker or How to Get a British Passport
author: Samson Kambalu
note: Kambalu is a gifted and funny writer who presents his life, and that of his father the Jive Talker, as an open book. Technically this isn't fiction but this Malawian Banda-era coming-of-age tale sure reads like it. In the tale, the 'jive talker' is the author's hustling alcoholic father who was known to riff bombastically on an encyclopedic range of subjects, a trait that obviously wore off on his son.

title: The Kafir of Khartala
author: Mohamed Toihiri
note: Unfortunately, there is no published English version of this novel--I was only able to read it because I asked for a copy from the professor who had translated it at the University of Vermont. Published in 2000 amidst one of Comoros' many coups, Toihiri has penned a wry, surprisingly racy novel that satirizes much of the politics and culture of his home country. At the heart of the story is a married doctor's affair with a younger French NGO teacher, and the doctor is dying from cancer.

title: The Sympathizer
author: Viet Thanh Nguyen
note: You get to view the Vietnam War from a totally different perspective--in this case, that of a South Vietnamese officer who is actually an American-educated spy for the communist North. Other themes addressed against the backdrop of the war include poverty, Hollywood "yellow-washing," torture, love-making and billiards, confession, forgetting and happiness as a zero-sum game in war.

title: They Are Coming
author: Christopher Mlalazi
note: You are wanting to read something from Zimbabwe. Look, this wasn't the best thing I've ever read but it was an important read because it forced me to think of the idea of family outside the normal western version. In this story a township family struggles as a rebellious daughter joins a local militia before the approaching 2005 parliamentary elections--it's about the locality of all politics but more so about what it means to grow up amidst uncertainty and violence.

title: Through the Eyes of a Lion
author: Levi Lusko
note: This is a book about unspeakable loss but it's also a book that offers you a plan--not only for yourself but also for comforting others. This is one of those books that you might not really want to read for fear of some type of psychic pain by association, but you will be glad you did.

title: American Rust
author: Philipp Meyer
note: I first encountered Meyer's writing back in 2015 by way of S.C. Gwynne's incredible Empire of the Summer Moon, which led me to Meyer's The Son. That led me to Meyer's first novel, American Rust, which reads like a sorrowful swan song to the American rust belt, drawing the reader into the lives of several families struggling to love, survive, and escape as the steel industry dies around them. My only critique is that I wished Meyer had wrapped up the story a little more neatly.

title: Artemis
author: Andy Weir
note: I read author Andy Weir's blockbuster debut novel The Martian back in 2015 and loved it, so when Artemis came out I added it to my Audible queue. Artemis is a colony on the moon, and the story focuses on a young smuggler named Jazz, raised on the moon, who develops a pirat-ish code of conduct aimed at moving up the stratified social order. Jazz irked me at times--she comes across as a little too enamored with just how cute and clever she is--but Weir has done what talented authors do: create a previously unknown world and open its door to readers everywhere.

title: Chaos Monkeys
author: Antonio García Martínez
note: Chaos Monkeys is a book bulging with so much saucy insider detail that you feel a little guilty reading it. Readers will find it of particular value if they are looking to get into the weeds of starting their own company, launching an idea, or courting investors. Beyond that, there was much lacking in Martinez' life choices in my personal opinion--don't expect to finish the book feeling great about the story, which is more an indictment of the rather sordid way things are done in Silicon Valley than of the author himself.

title: Deep Work
author: Cal Newport
summary: This was one of the most useful books that I have ever read. My full review is titled 'Read Deep Work Because It Will Change Everything.' Newport makes the case that the ability to focus without distraction on cognitively demanding tasks is one of the most valuable and increasingly rare skills in the modern economy.

title: Essentialism: The Discipline Pursuit of Less
author: Greg McKeown
note: Essentialism is a book for anyone who feels overwhelmed at any level of their life. The author's contention is that we all have too much going on in our lives, at work, at play, in our relationships and in our things, and this book gives the reader the tools and philosophical background to pare away the excess. Ultimately, McKeown espouses the expansion of designer Dieter Ram's mantra "less but better" into one's life at every level.

title: Hawaii
author: James A. Michener
note: In 2009, I married my beautiful bride on Poipu Beach in Kauai, and since then the Hawaiian islands have held a special place in my heart. First published in 1959, Hawaii comes across a little dated in style but Michener nonetheless proves he can craft an engrossing tale as he spans the physical birth of the Hawaiian islands eons ago up to its fight for statehood, juxtaposing generations of earnest missionaries with lusty whalers and traders. One assumes Michener took a multitude of historical liberties, but upon finishing it I was okay with that.

title: Hidden Christmas
author: Tim Keller
note: Tim Keller stepped down as senior pastor at the renowned Redeemer Church in NYC in early 2017, the church he built into 5000 strong members in one of the most unchurched areas of the United States. Hidden Christmas is the product of a lifetime of Keller's Christmas sermons, covering the breadth of the Christmas story from its roots in the Old Testament. I finished it with a newfound understanding for just how significant it was that God became man--that all that had to happen for the people of Israel to approach God was to merely come, and adore him, in a manger, as a defenseless baby.

title: Moving the Palace
author: Charif Majdalani
note: Majdalani's Moving the Palace is one of those books that leaves you melancholy and blue because it's over, and your relationship with the protagonist is severed forever, buried in the pages of the novel. We learn the story of Samuel, an erudite Christian adventurer who leaves his homeland of Lebanon amidst the great migration of the early 20th century and ventures down to the Sudan, where he encounters a small Arabian palace and hatches a plan of an origin his grandson can only guess at.

title: Prodigal God
author: Tim Keller
summary: First off, as an English major I am embarrassed to admit that I did not know the correct definition of the word prodigal. I always thought it referred to someone who screws up and comes back around, hat in hand. I couldn't be further from the truth — prodigal means someone who is wastefully extravagant, who spends their resources recklessly and freely. So in this story what you really have is a parable about two lost sons and a reckless (with his love) father. Keller's reorientation of the story from its traditional focus on the wayward son gives one more accurate insight into just who this tale was for. In context, Jesus' main audience was the 'older brothers' — the self-righteous pharisees who sought to control their own salvation through rigorous adherence to the law. One of best parts of Prodigal God is that it turns the reader outward showing that recognition of God's costly grace should spur his followers to serve the poor, to love the orphans and widows in society today. On my yearly re-reading list.
quotes: Prodigal means someone who is wastefully extravagant, who spends their resources recklessly and freely.
The lavish and extravagant call of the Father to sons and daughters of all types—to join him in the banquet.

title: Sleepwalking Land
author: Mia Couto
note: Mia Couto has crafted a surreal, engrossing tragic tale of the hopelessness of life during the fifteen year long civil war that consumed post-independence Mozambique. Couto focuses on pulling the reader into the psyche of those affected most by war--the ones not doing the killing--the women and the children. Early on, the narrator relates one of the most bizarre stories I've ever read: his younger brother is forced to live in the family's henhouse by their insane father, and forced to act like a chicken, for so long that he eventually becomes one and disappears.

title: The Hard Thing About Hard Things
author: Ben Horowitz
note: This book is on just about every entrepreneur's reading list. While much of the discussion is tech-focused, it would serve most military leaders well because Horowitz devotes much of his time to addressing how best to cope with the loneliness of leadership and "command." The most useful section for me came in chapter five: "Take Care of the People, The Products, and the Profits, In That Order." For any military leader planning to transition to the civilian workforce, this book should feature prominently on his or her reading list prior to retirement.
quote: Take Care of the People, The Products, and the Profits, In That Order.

title: The Last Will and Testament of Senor da Silva Araujo
author: Germano Almeida
note: This translated piece of Cape Verdean fiction is beautiful, sad, and meandering as it examines the life of a poor peasant boy made good as a successful business owner. Senor da Silva's life unfolds in a non-linear, often-dreamlike fashion as we follow him through the reading of his last will and testament. Far more interesting, though, is the investigation by da Silva's illegitimate daughter into a mysterious love obsession named Adelia. As the details are slowly revealed, a melancholy shadow falls over da Silva as he grapples with love, rejection and loss.

title: The Leader's Bookshelf
author: Adm. James Stavridis & R. Manning Ancell
note: Jim Stavridis' gift to bookavores everywhere, The Leader's Bookshelf is difficult to take notes on because it's so packed with great and essential material. Stavridis and former Navy PAO R. Ancell put together a Top 50 reading list for military leaders by surveying hundreds of senior military leaders, then ranked the most frequently cited titles by frequency of citation. As a FAO, I enjoyed Stavridis' analyses that repeatedly noted the need for regional expertise to better inform national strategic decision-making.

title: The Twelve Lives of Samuel Hawley
author: Hannah Tinti
note: Let me just get it out of the way and say that Chapter 15 will break your heart. The Twelve Lives of Samuel Hawley is a modern day adaptation of the ancient Greek tale of the 12 Labors of Hercules, and the genius of author Hannah Tinti's story is that she layers several tragic love stories onto this epic framework. Mechanically, the novel overlays the history of the 12 bullets that struck Samuel Hawley during his life with his daughter Lou's own coming of age, as she slowly uncovers her father's sordid criminal past.

title: The Warmth of Other Suns
author: Isabel Wilkerson
summary: Read it Because: You can't afford not to. Before the publication of Warmth some seven years ago, there had never been a definitive and comprehensive analysis of these waves of migration that began following the reconstruction era as Jim Crow set in. Let the following statement sink in: Just 77 years ago, in 1/3 of the US, killing blacks was not a crime. Now while I may not agree with every single tenet of the #blacklivesmatter movement, reading this book opened my eyes to the historical headspace from which its leaders and followers are coming. Much of the conflict in today's society would at least be softened if both sides took the time to know one another's history. Wilkerson has presented a gift to Americans.
quotes: Just 77 years ago, in 1/3 of the US, killing blacks was not a crime.

title: Transit
author: Abdourahman A. Waberi*
note: As you can probably guess there's not exactly a ton of Djibouti fiction out there, especially en anglais, but when it comes to Djibouti fiction, the author Waberi reigns supreme. Transit interweaves the story of Bashir, an uneducated ex-soldier, with that of Harbi, a member of the opposition intellectual elite, as the two men reveal their life stories while waiting at the Paris airport. The staying power of this story comes from Waberi's deft touch as he tackles the complexity of his country's history and current political situation through alternating monologues.

title: Vakio Milamina
author: Soamiely Andriamananjara
note: It gives you the inside scoop on the culture and politics of Madagascar. Soamiely is the rare Malagasy author who writes in (great) English about these things. Vakio Milamina lays out the author's thoughts on life in Madagascar through a series of very loosely related essays. In particular I loved "It's All Part of Growing Up," basically a 6 page rundown on the political history of Madagascar, and "Our Unambiguously Ambiguous Art of Conversation," in which he lays out why a Malagasy will seldom ever say 'no.'

title: African Kaiser
author: Robert Gaudi
note: It quickly becomes apparent in this impressive tome that author Robert Gaudi is a bit of a von Lettow-Vorbeck fanboy, but the admiration isn't without merit--the reader learns of the insurmountable odds faced by the German general fighting alone, without logistical support, in German East Africa during World War I. He ended his four-year struggle in unconditional surrender, but completed his military service as a victor, having pushed Great Britain to expend immense treasure and forces pursuing his small army of guerrilla fighters. This lengthy book flies by on the strength of Gaudi's story-telling.

title: American War: A Novel
author: Omar El Akkad
note: Egyptian-Canadian journalist-turned-author Omar El Akkad has penned a tale of future-historical fiction, dropping us into a world 40 years from now where America has erupted into conflict over the federal government's outlawing of fossil fuels, aggravated by accelerated global warming and rising ocean levels. We witness the tragic arc of Sarat, a young girl whose life is thrown into chaos as war encroaches on her family, and who becomes a hardened sniper, then prisoner of war, then mass murderer. The novel's staying power comes from Akkad's mastery in capturing how media and political rhetoric can distort reality and inflame a populace.

title: Beyond the Rice Fields
author: Naivo
note: Malagasy author Naivo has crafted a heart-wrenching tale of love set amidst one slave's seemingly impossible yearning for success and upward mobility. Impressively, this expansive piece of pre-colonial historical fiction doesn't hold back in addressing taboo subjects in Madagascar such as slavery and the wholesale execution of Christians under Queen Ranavalona's reign in the 19th century. The book reads as a mixture of hainteny (oral tale/poetry) and tantara (historical narrative) with a liberal dosing of Malagasy proverbs--I counted 29 of them.

title: Dancing in the Glory of Monsters
author: Jason Stearns
note: I listened to this lengthy journalistic endeavor by writer Jason Stearns as he attempts to unravel the most complicated conflict the world never cared about: the two wars in the Congo. As Stearns digs deeper you are quickly struck by the overwhelming intensity of violence--the sheer level of sexual violence is incomprehensible, and there's likely no one in the country of 64 million who doesn't know someone who was raped or assaulted. Ultimately, it is the absence of any justice or effective state institutions that marks the Congo's conflicts as distinct from those elsewhere in Africa.

title: Every Good Endeavor: Connecting Your Work to God's Work
author: Tim Keller
note: Pastor Tim Keller's Every Good Endeavor is not a beach read--it's a hunker-down-with-a-highlighter-and-pen examination of what work means to God and what it should mean to anyone following God, laying out the Bible's answers to why we want to work, why it's so hard, and how the story of Jesus' life leads us to find satisfaction in it. Ultimately, Keller argues that more than any marches, protests, or online petitions, our work is our greatest opportunity to shape culture.

title: Memoirs of a Porcupine
author: Alain Mabanckou
note: This is a difficult novel from renowned author Alain Mabanckou. Hailing from the Republic of Congo, Mabanckou has penned a pointed tale aimed at taking down the role of backwoods superstition amidst modernity--at least that's what really smart people say he was doing. I will admit I didn't really understand the point of this novel and had to do some research to come to that conclusion. I was pretty disappointed I hadn't picked a different Congolese novel, but I later discovered the book won the 2006 Renaudot prize, so I've decided to give it a second chance, at least on an intellectual level.

title: Sing, Unburied, Sing
author: Jesmyn Ward
note: Early on, the young son of a barely-there, back-country, voodoo-meth-addicted mother makes the damning pronouncement that his mother "kills things," and indeed death hangs heavy in this novel. Ostensibly, this is a fairly straightforward narrative: a single mother, her two children, and her junkie friend embark on a road trip to pick up her boyfriend when he gets out of jail. The story's depth emerges as Ward reveals that three of the characters can see wandering dead ghosts looking for answers, and she's created characters with whom she so deeply empathizes that their visions seem real and necessary rather than fantastical.

title: The Beautiful Things that Heaven Bears
author: Dinaw Mengestu
summary: Before Adichie was regaling the world with her story of a relatively privileged immigrant experience in 'Americanah', Ethiopian author Dinaw Mengestu was pulling back the curtain on a much bleaker immigrant experience. The title comes from the closing lines of Dante's Inferno. In the case of the narrator Sepha and his friends, they have escaped the violence in their own country and arrived in America, 'seeing the stars.' Only they soon discover that while their new life in the United States may not be hell, it will likely be much more like the penance of purgatory than that of paradiso.
quotes: As it was, I did not come to America to find a better life. I came here running and screaming with the ghosts of an old one firmly attached to my back.
All the best dictators are colonels.

title: The Gunny Sack
author: M.G. Vassanji
note: Most of the African fiction that I've read has focused on the "native" experience, so I found this to be an important novel as it focuses exclusively on the experience of four generations of an Indian family in Tanzania and wider East Africa beginning in the late 1800s. Vassanji relates this in-depth experience across the span of Tanzania's colonization and eventual independence through the backward lens of the narrator Kala, who parses through the contents of a gunny sack bequeathed to him by his great grandmother.

title: The Memory of Love
author: Aminattah Forna
note: "We all were happy here once," reminisces Kai, the local Freetown surgeon, after the death of his last link to the time before. With beautiful, striking prose, author Aminatta Forna reveals the soul of a nation where nearly everyone is stricken with what the West calls PTSD, but as one local notes, "You call it a disorder, my friend. We call it life." Forna does not give in to easy storylines about the courage of the war's survivors--it is the memory of Sierra Leone's before that gives people the strength to slowly put their lives together again.
quote: You call it a disorder, my friend. We call it life.

title: Turn the Ship Around
author: David Marquet
note: I let this book rest dormant on my Amazon wishlist for months because of my unfair bias against submariners, but I wasn't even a chapter in when I realized how misguided that bias had been. Midway through, retired Captain David Marquet shares the story of a nighttime passage through the strait of Malacca that nearly resulted in a collision, averted by the timely actions of one of his petty officers--whom Marquet immediately awarded a Navy Achievement Medal on the spot. This floored me, since in my own experience recognition like that almost always arrives a year late, if at all.

title: What the Day Owes the Night
author: Yasmina Khadra
summary: This book is a perfect example of why reading fiction is important. Reading fiction like this forces the reader to internalize and grapple with historical events in a way that a straightforward history never could. In this case, by personalizing something like the Algerian independence struggle, the reader must reevaluate the dry numbers, facts and statistics littering the historical record and connect them to the actual people and struggles behind them.
quotes: Have you ever dared, Younes, even once in your life?
If a woman loves you, no star is beyond your grasp, no god can touch you.
Only love can make good the misfortunes and evils of the world.

title: Beacon 23
author: Hugh Howey
note: Hugh Howey takes us for a ride with a PTSD'd alien-fighting grunt turned interstellar lighthouse keeper. Howey imagines a future in which "lighthouse beacons" are placed amidst warp speed preventing asteroid belts--lighthouse keepers control a device that, when activated, allows ships to pass through the asteroid belts without collision. Without that device activated, any passing ship will be obliterated. Couple this setting with a pending alien invasion and a possible love interest and you've got a great story you'll devour.

title: Beneath the Lion's Gaze
author: Maaza Mengiste
note: One of the only African nations never to have been colonized, one of Ethiopia's darkest periods came during the 13-year socialist rule of the Derg. This military junta overthrew an increasingly oblivious Haile Selassie and set about consolidating power under the leadership of despot Mengistu, and Beneath the Lion's Gaze retells this period through the eyes of one Ethiopian family. The family's patriarch is a renowned doctor ordered to keep a barely-alive torture victim alive so she can be tortured further; his son becomes a freedom fighter known as "Mekonnen Killer of Soldiers." Author Maaza Mengiste uses this one family's struggles to bring to life the experience of Ethiopians who starved under Selassie, only to be persecuted and killed under the Derg and starved again under Mengistu's reign.
quote: Mekonnen Killer of Soldiers.

title: Between the World and Me
author: Ta-Nehisi Coates
note: This is a love letter from a father to a son, written with the urgency of a man who knows he may perish at any moment. Coates writes beautifully as he recalls his youth and experiences as a father--his memoir reflects serious inner self-examination as he seeks to create a blueprint for his son to survive, and possibly even thrive, in a world Coates argues is stacked against him from a historical, institutional, and psycho-cultural perspective. As Coates warns his son to keep his guard up, he adds: "Never forget that we were enslaved in this country longer than we have been free."
quote: Never forget that we were enslaved in this country longer than we have been free.

title: Ghost Fleet
author: P.W. Singer & August Cole
note: The premise is that sometime in the near future China, partnered with Russia, conducts a cyber/space/electronic warfare attack that gives them dominance across the warfare spectrum. Our fleet and air assets are near completely destroyed, and with all our U.S. satellites destroyed, the United States no longer has freedom of movement on land, air, or sea. The heart of the story is how the U.S. fights back--a combination of insurgency in the occupied Hawaiian islands, a reconstitution of mothballed ships and aircraft, a private-public wartime industrial partnership, and a murky collaboration with Anonymous hackers. The story moves quickly, and I burned through the pages as the authors keep the narrative moving with quick dialogue, myriad Sun-Tzu quotes, and sympathetic characters.

title: Gratitude in Low Voices
author: Dawit Gebremichael Habte
note: Equal parts memoir, history and adventure novel, Dawit Habte's book is the story of not only his country's 91-year struggle for independence but also his own incredible journey from a countryside village in Eritrea to the offices of Bloomberg as a software engineer. As with many refugee stories that have come out of Africa, the western reader will be floored by the tenacity, resilience and grit the author displays at such a young age as he smuggles himself out of Ethiopia and finally gets refugee status to start his life over in the United States. One thing that struck me was his commentary on the dehumanizing nature of seeking asylum--where you are assumed to be starting anew on a better life despite having a family, people, and country you call home half a world away.

title: Hillbilly Elegy
author: J.D. Vance
note: I read J.D. Vance's 2016 memoir of a family and culture in crisis in less than two days. Vance is just a flat-out great writer as he tells the story of his life growing up between Ohio and Kentucky along the redneck Rt. 23. His basic argument is that one of the major issues plaguing Appalachian America is that people there feel hopeless and disenfranchised, not just politically but in all facets of life--in short, they feel like their choices don't matter. In Vance's case, the only way he was able to rise above these circumstances was the reliable and persistent presence of a family member throughout his immediate family's tumultuous arc. Oh, that and a stint in the Marine Corps.

title: Homegoing
author: Yaa Gyasi
note: This masterpiece of historical fiction follows the lives of two separated half-sisters, and their six generations of descendants, beginning in the mid-1700s in the area of West Africa known today as Ghana. Gyasi uses their two very different paths to highlight not only the horrors of the slave trade but also the bravery and determination of the many who persevered and survived across the span of time. Ultimately, the 25-year-old author accomplishes a breath-taking feat in creating a compelling history of slavery told through 14 sequential short stories you'll likely never forget.

title: Mister Drainpipe
author: Marc Secchia
note: Author Marc Secchia took a hiatus from his slew of successful fantasy novels to pen this heartfelt love letter to the homeless of Addis Ababa. I tore through this book in less than two days, and I'll warn you that this story will pierce even the hardest heart and, hopefully, change the way you think about and interact with the homeless and "least" in your daily life.

title: Prevail: The Inspiring Story of Ethiopia's Victory Over Mussolini's Invasion
author: Jeff Pearce
note: I can't imagine there's a more well-researched book out there on Ethiopia's decades-long resistance and eventual defeat of an overeager, over confident, overreaching wanna-be colonial power. Canadian author Jeff Pearce's work is strongest as he brings to life the myriad actors, antagonists, and heroes of the saga. It's worth noting that Pearce is a bit of a Selassie apologist, but given the complicated history of Ras Tafari Makkonen's reign, I imagine this can fall either way.

title: Rock Breaks Scissors
author: William Poundstone
note: The main point I took away from this novel was that people are crummy at trying to be "random." All of this book's applications are founded on one simple idea: when people make arbitrary, random, or strategic choices, they fall into unconscious patterns that you can predict. While entertaining to read, I'd say most of his writing is only useful for specific circumstances like taking multiple choice tests, office pools, rock-paper-scissors, catching fraud, and buying a home.

title: Season of the Shadow
author: Léonora Miano
note: The story begins as the inhabitants of a tribal village tucked away in the interior of modern day Cameroon awaken to find its huts ablaze and 12 of its men vanished. This mysterious disappearance sets off a chain of events that, as one might guess, ends in tragedy. The tale's power comes as the reader is placed in the middle of a people group whose whole order and existence is thrown into havoc--even as the reader is keenly aware of what actually happened, the kidnapping is so out of place with centuries of accepted conduct that the village's own members can't fathom who the perpetrators might be.

title: The Shadow King
author: Maaza Mengiste
summary: Following Mussolini's invasion to avenge Italy's 19th-century embarrassment at the Battle of Adua, Kidane assembles a local militia at exiled Emperor Haile Selassie's behest. His wife Aster leads the women who trail the fighters supplying food and bullets, and eventually convinces him to let her women fight. Mengiste brings to light a forgotten piece of Ethiopia's resistance against Italy — the women who fought and were written out of the history. Her talent has continued to grow since her debut, evident in stunning lines throughout.
quotes: They have come to rewrite history, to alter memory, to resurrect their dead and refashion them as heroes.
She is one woman. She is many women. She is all the sound that exists in the world.
We were the Shadow King. We were those who stepped into a country left dark by an invading plague and gave new hope to Ethiopia's people.

title: Small Country
author: Gaël Faye
note: Reading Gael Faye's Small Country, I was reminded of the writing of Teju Cole, Dinaw Mengestu, Maaza Mengiste, and NoViolet Bulawayo. Faye writes with a musician's flair, penning lines like "when he laughed, happiness washed over the walls of Mamie's small living room like a fresh lick of paint." Gabriel is Rwandan-French but spends his entire childhood in neighboring Burundi until the effects of the 1994 Rwanda genocide push him even further away to France, and it's there that he considers what exactly he's lost.
quote: I used to think I was exiled from my country. But, in retracing the steps of my past, I have understood that I was exiled from my childhood. Which seems so much crueler.

title: Strong Fathers, Strong Daughters: 10 Secrets Every Father Should Know
author: Meg Meeker
note: I first read this book back in 2015 and it's so important to fathers that I put it on my yearly re-reading list, although it took me four years to actually read it again. In my second reading it proved just as powerful, profound and insightful. The bottom line is that you can't underestimate the role of a father in his daughters' lives--you have to fight for her, engage with her, and continually draw her close throughout her life, particularly during those teen years. Don't worry about what other parents do; worry about your daughter.

title: The Dry
author: Jane Harper
note: In a dying Australian farm town, teetering on the edge of collapse due to droughts, a family is found dead in an apparent murder-suicide. An exiled man-turned-white-collar cop returns for the funeral and finds out his childhood best friend is the assumed killer of his own wife and kids. Did his friend do it? What secrets is the cop hiding? What secrets has the town buried? This was a page turner that kept me guessing till the end. A great quick read.

title: The Furious Longing of God
author: Brennan Manning
note: You can get through the 136 pages in one sitting if you'd like, but I think it's a book that's best consumed in spurts with time to mull over Brennan Manning's meditation on God's love for us. This book likely has a message for any reader, most importantly this: "Our religion never begins with what we do for God. It always starts with what God has done for us."
quote: Our religion never begins with what we do for God. It always starts with what God has done for us.

title: The Heart of War: Misadventures in the Pentagon
author: Kathleen J. McInnis
note: McInnis' razor sharp, satirical story of a young, idealist, peacenik PhD's foray into the Pentagon brought me back to my days inside the 5-sided torture chamber. It's a novel about one woman's evolution from skeptic to true(ish) believer as she discovers that life inside is nothing like she expected.

title: The High Mountains of Portugal
author: Yann Martel
note: Man Booker prize winning author Yann Martel has crafted a trio of related novellas that lay bare the trauma of loss and its psychic weight that can echo across generations like a deep curse. We join the journey of three characters: a bereaved widower on a mission to find a lost African crucifix; a grieving, delusional pathologist obsessed with Agatha Christie; and an aging widower/politician who buys a chimpanzee and retreats with the animal to his family's ancestral village.

title: The Three Body Problem
author: Cixin Liu
note: This is a trippy novel that's not easily summarized, and it's easy to get lost in the more technical aspects that Cixin explores in depth (nanotechnology, AI, metaphysics, quantum mechanics), particularly when listening to it in 45 minute blocks on Audible in D.C. traffic over a month's time. The basic premise is that a technologically superior alien civilization has decided to colonize earth.

title: To Stop a Warlord: My Story of Justice, Grace, and the Fight for Peace
author: Shannon Sedgwick Davis
note: This book is the story of how one woman created a new paradigm in how NGOs and charitable organizations should, could, and can confront atrocity and evil by partnering with the military. Shannon Davis is the CEO of Bridgeway Foundation, and she decided to do something about Joseph Kony and his reign of terror.

title: Where the Crawdads Sing
author: Delia Owens
note: I read this beautiful lyrical book in two days, no exaggeration. It's equal parts murder mystery, searing social indictment, love letter to naturalism, and soaring poetry--amazing that this is author Delia Owens' first work of fiction.

title: Where the Light Gets In: Losing My Mother Only To Find Her Again
author: Kimberly Williams-Paisley
note: A candid, emotional memoir of a daughter's struggles, hopes, triumphs, and losses as she copes with her mother's primary progressive aphasia, a rare form of dementia. The story is about more than the disease itself--it's about the bond between daughters and their mothers.

title: American Spy
author: Lauren Wilkinson
note: This novel is both a fictionalized telling of Burkina Faso President Sankara's downfall and a commentary on what it means to be a black woman in both an American and African society. The protagonist is a black female FBI agent named Marie who is haunted by her sister's death and stifled by the lack of opportunity in her office. When she gets a chance to become a real spy she dives into it but soon realizes that many of her assumptions may have been wrong.

title: Being Mortal
author: Atul Gawande
summary: Early on author Atul Gawande captures the decision that we will all make (ideally) before we grow old: 'how to make life worth living when we're weak and frail and can't fend for ourselves anymore.' More specifically, he seeks to find out how people can age without having to choose between neglect and institutionalization. Most frightening is his conclusion that for the majority of the medical community 'hope is not a plan, but it is our plan.'
quotes: Hope is not a plan, but it is our plan.

title: Blessed Child
author: Ted Dekker & Bill Bright
note: The premise is that an American baby named Caleb is given to an Ethiopian monastery after his mother is murdered by invading Eritrean forces. Ten years later his life is in jeopardy and a Peace Corps worker and Red Cross nurse rescue him and take him to the U.S., where we discover that Caleb can heal people and that senior politicians want him dead.

title: Burn-In
author: P.W. Singer & August Cole
note: In this future-historical fiction, the writing duo tackles a future in which AI, drones and robotics have continued their current development trajectory to the point where they've consumed every aspect of American life. Unfortunately, these "developments" have left gaping vulnerabilities within the US infrastructure that are exploited by a group of homegrown terrorists.

title: By Night the Mountains Burn
author: Juan Tomás Ávila Laurel
note: This novel takes place during the colonial era on one of Equatorial Guinea's neglected islands--the country itself is run from its capital city Malabo on its largest island, Bioko. A frequent critic of dictator Obiang, the self-exiled author tells a tale of a marginalized community that survives, sometimes, on the narrowest of margins, depending on passing European ships and a strange periodic washing ashore of squids.

title: Call Sign Chaos
author: Jim Mattis & Bing West
summary: This book has been long anticipated and it delivers. General Mattis writes just as he leads — candid and thoughtful. This book strikes a delicate balance between an autobiography and a leadership book. Mattis and co-author Bing West utilize Mattis' career as a roadmap to illustrate leadership at three different levels: Direct, Executive, and Strategic. The one theme he kept coming back to was unleashing others' initiative. Required reading for any military officer or anyone in a leadership position.
quotes: If you haven't read hundreds of books, you are functionally illiterate, and you will be incompetent, because your personal experiences alone aren't broad enough to sustain you.
A leader's role is problem solving. If you don't like problems, stay out of leadership.

title: Death Be Not Proud
author: John Gunther
note: The name of this 1949 memoir was taken from English poet John Donne's poem of the same title. In it the author and father John Gunther recounts the life and death of his son who passed after a struggle with a malignant brain tumor.
quote: A mournful tribute not only to Johnny but to the power, the wealth, the unconquerable beauty of the human spirit, will, and soul.

title: Designing Your Life: A Framework For Building A Life You Can Thrive In
author: Bill Burnett & Dave Evans
summary: Stanford design professors Burnett and Evans apply design thinking to the challenge of building a life. The core insight is to stop asking 'what do I want to do with my life' (a gravity problem — unchangeable) and start prototyping multiple possible lives to see what fits. Essential reading for anyone navigating a career transition.

title: Diamonds, Gold, and War: The British, the Boers, and the Making of South Africa
author: Martin Meredith
note: This book traces the origin of South Africa and the discovery of gold and diamonds there that sparked off a bloody conflict with Britain that ultimately led to the consolidation and foundation of the apartheid republic that existed until 1994. This detailed history should be mandatory reading for any budding Africanist.

title: Don't Save Anything
author: James Salter
summary: Salter's wife put together a collection of his essays and articles and entitled it 'Don't Save Anything'. The title comes from Salter's long time advice 'don't save anything' — meaning don't reserve any writing ideas or details for a later piece, rather pour everything you have into each piece. It's a fitting title representing some of the best writing from Salter, the authors' author. In particular, I was struck by the Salter's terrific profile pieces in the book — I've never read such masterful writing in this genre. Reading them made me wish he'd done biographies.
quotes: In the end, writing is like a prison, an island from which you will never be released but which is a kind of paradise: the solitude, the thoughts, the incredible joy of putting into words the essence of what you for the moment understand and with your whole heart want to believe.
You cannot teach someone to write any more than you can teach them to be interesting.

title: Educated
author: Tara Westover
note: Educated is the story of how one Mormon-ish girl overcomes a childhood under the reign of an undiagnosed bi-polar, off-the-grid father who believed the "end times" are imminent and enforced his own brand of extremist Mormon values upon his family.

title: Lives Other Than My Own
author: Emmanuel Carrère
note: In this memoir, French author Emmanuel Carrere takes issue with Fitzgerald's well known assertion that "all life is a process of breaking down" as he peers into the life of Juliette, carefully building a picture of her circling journey from tragedy to love to passion and back to tragedy as her cancer returns to deal its final blow.
quote: That is the worst of fates: never to have been seen, never to have been acknowledged.

title: Mrs. Bridge
author: Evan S. Connell
note: With a careful eye and razor sharp bite, author Connell captures a period in American history for the well-heeled social strata, decades before the sexual revolution, feminism, the Vietnam War, and the Civil Rights movement.

title: She Would Be King
author: Wayétu Moore
note: In her debut novel Wayetu Moore channels the idea of lawful magic as she joins magical realist authors like Couto, Martel, and Marquez in telling an alternative origin story of the Pepper Coast lands that would become Liberia.

title: That Will Never Work
author: Marc Randolph
note: In this book, equal parts origin story, startup manifesto, and leadership manual, author Marc Randolph recounts how Netflix grew from an idea bandied about in a beaten-up Volvo to a $194B global behemoth.

title: The Art of Fiction
author: James Salter
note: In his inaugural lecture as a UVA Kapnick Distinguished Writer-in-Residence, Salter offered that "books are passwords"--the idea that among those who are well read, books are a connection, a filial link of brotherhood amidst theretofore strangers.

title: The Insanity of God (
author: Nik Ripken
note: At one point author Nikk Ripken's NGO was the ONLY ONE operating in Somalia--his team of 8 was feeding 50,000 refugees a day and running mobile health clinics around the country.
quote: I'd never met a believer in persecution that wasn't running for their life. In those years in Somalia, we had 150 followers of Jesus in the country. When we left, only four were left alive.

title: The Red-Haired Woman
author: Orhan Pamuk
note: On the surface the novel is about a boy serving as a well apprentice in post-WWII Turkey, two possible murders, one case of possible incest, and late 20th century capitalism in the country.

title: The Submission
author: Amy Waldman
note: This 2011 American Book Award Winner is a backwards look at the psyche and soul of an American public that will never stop grieving 9-11.

title: American Dirt
author: Jeanine Cummins
note: This is a brutal, violent, and necessary novel. Author Jeanine Cummins holds nothing back as she pulls the reader into the suffering and anguish that propels countless migrants to make the dangerous journey.

title: Behold the Dreamers
author: Mbolo Mbue
note: The novel's power comes in the author's ability to capture both the strengths and the hollowness of the promise of America. The reader sees the rise and good fortune of Jende as his chauffeur position gives him relative wealth and burgeoning opportunities for his family.

title: Born a Crime: Tales from a South African Childhood
author: Trevor Noah
note: You may know Trevor Noah as the host of the Daily Show, but long before he entered the mainstream he was a derelict kid born to a black Xhosa mother and a white Swiss father--one who appeared "colored" to those around him but who was raised in the Xhosa culture.

title: Cocktail Hour Under the Tree of Forgetfulness
author: Alexandra Fuller
note: Emily appreciated the story's honesty and eye-opening look at both the beauty and sorrow of life. The author shared a unique perspective of a white girl living in Africa during the period of political independence.

title: Homeland Elegies
author: Ayad Akhtar
summary: Pulitzer-winning author Ayad Akhtar charges that America is a 'buffer solution' that 'keeps things together but always separated' in his unforgettable novel-cum-memoir. DoDReads published my full review.
quotes: America is a buffer solution that keeps things together but always separated.

title: I'm Still Here: Black Dignity in a World Made for Whiteness
author: Austin Channing Brow
note: An eye-opening, and heart-opening, look at one woman's experiences growing up and working in "a world made for whiteness." She writes beautifully and with a vulnerability and urgency that asks the reader to wake up and cast aside complacency.
quote: I need a love that is troubled by injustice...a love that has no tolerance for hate, no excuses for racist decisions, no contentment in the status quo.

title: Lion Mountain
author: Mustapha Tlili
