note: In the story, famed Tunisian author Mustapha Tlili paints a secluded village modeled on Feriana, the home of his youth. It's an ancient place lost amidst the desert steppes but which has persisted through the centuries, sheltered always under the shoulder of Lion Mountain.
quote: A geography more than physical or human: sacred...landmarks determining the rights of each and every individual were perfectly clear.

title: Missionaries
author: Phil Klay
summary: Reading Marine veteran Phil Klay's new novel forces readers to step back and consider the 'progress' and globalization of American warfare since 9-11. The novel accomplishes this by examining the decades-long Colombian civil war through the converging lives of a weary American war-zone journalist, an American Special Forces Officer, a career Colombian Army officer, and an orphaned Colombian village boy-turned-killer. DoDReads published my full review.
quotes: Wars are not fought by armies. They are fought by cultures. Your job is not to trim the claws. It's to kill the beast.
In all his time watching Yemenis die on video screens, he had not once talked to a single Yemeni, or even seen one in person. They were a notional people to him.

title: Project Hail Mary
author: Andy Weir
note: Martian author Andy Weir returns to outer space with a tale that is equal parts nerdy and hokey as he introduces us to Ryland Grace, a failed scientist and reluctant hero. When scientists discover that the sun is slowly being sapped of its power, Grace finds himself in the middle of a race to save earth, and possibly the universe, from extinction.

title: Quiet Cadence
author: Mark Treanor
note: The novel's violence is unrelenting, random, and total. Its first death is a dead body literally flying through the air.
quote: Then the panic hit me: Do I have all my parts?

title: Raising Up a Generation of Third Culture Kids
author: Lauren Wells
note: While this book is targeted towards parents raising kids overseas, most of it applies to military families. The author posits that kids growing up as "third culture" often end up with enormous amounts of grief and trauma.

title: Steal Away Home: Charles Spurgeon and Thomas Johnson, Unlikely Friends on the Passage to Freedom
author: Matt Carter and Aaron Ivey
note: An incredible tale of friendship, faith and sacrifice. Charles Spurgeon is, of course, the renowned "prince of preachers." Authors Matt Carter and Aaron Ivey tell the story of his friendship with African-American missionary Thomas Johnson against the backdrop of Spurgeon's own struggles with depression, anxiety and gout.

title: Talking to Strangers
author: Malcom Gladwell
note: As a society we are not equipped to talk to strangers, so when things don't go as expected we blame the stranger as our immediate reflex. Instead we need to recognize our own limits to decipher and truly understand strangers, and instead embrace humility and restraint.

title: The Anomaly
author: Hervé Le Tellier
note: I first heard about this novel in a NYT article that mentioned the now available translation of the book that had enraptured France during the COVID lockdowns. The premise is that there's some sort of time-continuum split where a flight lands with the same people and same plane that had landed months earlier.

title: The Desert and the Drum
author: Mbarek Ould Beyrouk
note: "From the moment they arrived, the strangers stole something essential from us, without us feeling we had the right to protest." This comment by the young Bedouin narrator Rayhana encapsulates the modern-day struggle that Mauritanian author Mbarek Ould Beyrouk seeks to describe.
quote: From the moment they arrived, the strangers stole something essential from us, without us feeling we had the right to protest.

title: The Dissent Channel
author: Elizabeth Shackleford
note: During a Famous Rivers Africa Group session, we discussed this memoir with the author Lizzy Shackleton.

title: The Promise
author: Damon Galgut
note: Of course one isn't awarded the Booker prize for penning a surface-level story about a white family in South Africa. Rather Galgut has written a soaring multi-decade exposition on the promise of a post-Apartheid South Africa writ large.

title: What the Day Owes the Night
author: Yasmina Khadra
summary: This book is a perfect example of why reading fiction is important. Reading fiction like this forces the reader to internalize and grapple with historical events in a way that a straightforward history never could. In this case, by personalizing something like the Algerian independence struggle, the reader must reevaluate the dry numbers, facts and statistics littering the historical record and connect them to the actual people and struggles behind them.
quotes: Have you ever dared, Younes, even once in your life?
If a woman loves you, no star is beyond your grasp, no god can touch you.
Only love can make good the misfortunes and evils of the world.

title: A Caring Life
author: Aaron Santymire
note: I met author Aaron Santymire during my first embassy tour in Madagascar, where he served as a medical missionary. He's spent his entire adult life serving and caring for his family on the African continent, and this beautiful book is the accumulated outpouring of his wisdom.

title: An Italian Education
author: Tiim Parks
note: Published a quarter of a century ago, many of Tim Parks' observations still ring true in 2022 Italy. Chocked full of short vignettes that capture a Brit's experience raising his bi-national children in fair Verona.

title: How Beautiful We Were
author: Mbolo Mbue
note: "The problem with America is that it makes you dream"--this may well have been the last unspoken dying sigh of Thula, the protagonist in this second novel from Imbolo Mbue.
quote: The problem with America is that it makes you dream.

title: In the Name of Jesus: Reflections on Christian Leadership
author: Henri J.M. Nouwen
note: Father Henri Nouwen taught for 20 years at Harvard, Notre Dame, and Yale but began to feel his own spiritual death developing--what many Christian leaders may identify as burnout. So he prayed a simple, but very brave, prayer.
quote: Lord, show me where you want me to go and I will follow you, but please be clear and unambiguous about it!

title: Let My People Go Surfing: The Education of a Reluctant Businessman
author: Yvon Chouinard
note: Today everyone knows the name Patagonia, and on a cold winter day you can't go too far without seeing the familiar logo on someone's sweater. But nearly 50 years ago the global company was just what we'd today call a side hustle.

title: SuperNova Era
author: Ciu Lixin
note: While not as polished and developed as his later works, I tore through this quick-paced narrative in two days. The premise is intriguing--a supernova event occurs that kills off the entire adult population in a matter of months.

title: Taste: My Life Through Food
author: Stanley Tucci
note: This is Stanley Tucci's life told against the backdrop of food--it brought to mind another truly great food memoir, Life is Meals by James and Kay Salter, my favorite book by my favorite author to give as a housewarming gift.

title: The Fortune Men
author: Nadifa Mohamed
note: "Somalis have got the right idea, you wrong someone and you're forced to look over your shoulder for the rest of your life unless you make amends. You deal with each other face to face. Only cowards live by prisons and cold hangings."
quote: You wrong someone and you're forced to look over your shoulder for the rest of your life unless you make amends. Only cowards live by prisons and cold hangings.

title: The Gendarme
author: Mark Mustian
note: Its protagonist is an Ottoman gendarme who slaughtered Armenian men and women during the 1915 genocide. Along the way he also falls in love with one of the Armenian deportees, a love that remains unconsummated and seemingly unrequited over the course of more than 70 years.

title: The House of Sand and Fog
author: Andre Dubus III
note: My daughter bought this National Book Award finalist novel for me at a thrift store and it sat next to my bed for several months. When I finally started it, though, I finished it in days. Author Dubus can flat out write.

title: The Long Way Home: Discovering the Fullness of Life in the Father
author: Matt Carter
note: Pastor Matt Carter uses the biblical story of the prodigal son to grapple with the idea of faith in a broader church community where youth are struggling with depression in record numbers and leaving the church in droves once they reach adulthood.

title: The Power
author: Naomi Alderman
note: In this novel, author Naomi Alderman imagines a world in which the global patriarchal power structure is a buried memory. The novel unfolds retrospectively as historical research describing the roots of how women came to dominate all facets of society in this future world.

title: Transcendent Kingdom
author: Yaa Gyasi
note: In a departure from her grandiose debut novel Homegoing, here the author focuses on the life of one brilliant young scientist, a daughter of Ghanaian immigrants who struggles with her identity as a black woman and as the daughter of a mother struggling with depression and sister of a brother battling addiction.

title: Afterlives.
author: Abdulrazak Gurnah
note: Superb writing that traces the lives of three characters against the background of Tanzanian independence.

title: City of Dreams
author: Don Winslow
note: The second book in this crime writer's Danny Ryan series, building on the first.

title: City on Fire
author: Don Winslow
note: Master crime writer Don Winslow pens a fast-paced mafia thriller set in Providence, Rhode Island. The narrative focuses on Danny Ryan and his struggle to find a place between long-warring Italian and Irish families.

title: Dispatches From a Cowgirl: Through the Looking Glass With a Navy Diplomat's Wife
author: Julie Tully
note: Author Julie Tully pulls back the curtain on what it's like to be the wife of a diplomat living in Africa. With so much written about diplomats, attaches, and ambassadors, Tully fills an important void in telling the story of the vital spouses who form the foundation of life overseas.

title: Falling: A Novel
author: T.J. Newman
note: Came across this novel via author Don Winslow's twitter feed. This is a decent beach read thriller that takes the reader onto a plane that comes under an unconventional terrorist attack.

title: Into Thin Air.
author: Jon Krakauer
note: Tough, jaw-dropping read. My son got me this as a birthday present and I tore through it in a few short days.

title: Khalil
author: Yasmina Khadra
note: This 2021 novel takes the reader into the mind of a Moroccan/Belgian suicide bomber with his sights set on Paris. Notably, its author is a former Algerian Army officer living in exile in France who publishes under the pen name Yasmina Khadra.

title: Romantic Comedy: A Novel
author: Curtis Sittenfield
note: Superb beach read. I first read Sittenfeld back in 2006 and she struck me with her ability to create an encompassing and utterly believable boarding school atmosphere--a product of Woodberry Forest myself, I could attest to her mastery.

title: The Heartbeat of Wounded Knee: Native American from 1890 to Presen
author: David Treuer
note: My notes and quotes are on the blog, along with a separate 'poetic review.'

title: The Last Policeman.
author: Ben H. Winters
note: Winner of the Mystery Writers of America Edgar Award for best paperback original, the novel tells the story of a world facing pending destruction at the hand of a monster meteor.

title: The Love Songs of W.E.B. Du Bois
author: Honorée Fanonne Jeffers
summary: A sweeping, multi-generational saga that traces one Black American family across centuries, from West Africa to Georgia to the present day. One of the most ambitious American novels in years. My full review is on kruzoo.
quotes: She was owned, but her memories were not.
For the original transgression of this land was not slavery. It was greed, and it could not be contained.

title: The Midnight Library
author: Matt Haig
note: A quantum physics take on It's a Wonderful Life. Engrossing read that will have you second guessing every significant choice you've made in your own life.

title: The Murder Rule (Audible)
author: Dervla McTiernan
note: Great beach read! In early 2022 I took a screenshot of a tweet from crime writer Don Winslow recommending this book, forgot about it for a year, then downloaded it to my Libby app and finished it in 4 days.

title: The Overstory
author: Richard Powers
summary: I set the book down and became a raving lunatic about trees — the way they communicate and speak and nurture and repair one another. Powers does something fiction rarely does: he permanently altered the way I look at the world. I have since become 'that guy' who will not stop talking about trees. My full review is on kruzoo.
quotes: The best arguments in the world won't change a person's mind. The only thing that can do that is a good story.
Nothing is less isolated or more social than a tree.

title: The Passenger
author: Cormac McCarthy
note: Don't read Cormac McCarthy's entirely skippable book--180 pages of my life that I will never get back.

title: The Violin Conspiracy: A Novel
author: Brendan Slocumb
note: I came across this novel through Anne Bogel's podcast "What Should I Read Next?" in which she spoke with musician Brendan Slocumb about writing his first novel, a tale equal parts mystery and commentary on racism in America.

title: Tomorrow and Tomorrow and Tomorrow
author: Gabrielle Zevin
note: Loved this book! So many layers: video game design, love triangles, disability, friendship, companionship--the author's ability to weave so many major life themes into one arc gives it strong Gen Z great American novel vibes.

title: Uncertain Ground: Citizenship in an Age of Endless, Invisible War
author: Phil Klay
note: Such a great collection of essays.

title: A City in Ruins
author: Don Winslow
note: This is the third in the Danny Ryan trilogy by crime writer Don Winslow. Racing narrative--great beach read/listen.

title: A Promised Land
author: Barack Obama
summary: This memoir delivers — engrossing and full of insights into what a president goes through on a daily basis — from thought process, to congressional politics to family dynamics. No matter your politics you'll enjoy this humble and thoughtful retrospective. It ends right after they kill Osama so I can't wait for the next book! From a classmate's newsletter: 'What I look for in a memoir: Fun-to-read writing; laugh-out-loud humor; deep dives into the why of pivotal decisions. Conclusion: This book met and/or exceeded all of my criteria.'

title: Beartown
author: Fredrik Backman
note: Recommended to me as "Friday Night Lights" except with hockey--that's a pretty fair description for this fast-paced read. Set in the backwoods of a dying hockey town in Sweden, it's got sports, betrayal, and possibly murder--sports noir at its finest. And it's a trilogy!

title: Because of Winter
author: Nikki Brochetti
note: Amazing debut novel by author Nikki Brochetti.

title: Brazil: A Biography
author: Lilia M. Schwarcz & Heloisa M. Starling
note: Incredible Michael Lewis-style tale of the rise, and fall for some, of a billionaire class in Brazil, told against the backdrop of corruption, favelas, soccer stadiums, kidnappings and Miami real estate. This book offers keen insight into the Brazilian psyche and society writ large. Essential Brasil read.

title: Brazillionaires
author: Alex Cuadros
note: Incredible Michael Lewis-style tale of the rise of the 21st century rise (and fall for some) of a billionaire class in Brazil told against the backdrop of corruption, favelas, soccer stadiums, kidnappings and Miami real estate. This book offers keen insight into the Brazilian psyche and society writ large. Essential Brasil read.

title: Clandestine in Chile.
author: Gabriel García Márquez
note: Don't recommend. I really looked forward to this novel since I've always loved the magical realism of Gabriel Garcia Marquez, and while there's some beautiful writing, this journalistic retelling of a filmmaker's secret mission to film inside Chile and expose the brutality of life under Pinochet reads less like a spy novel and more like an ambling fait accompli.

title: Dancing with the Devil in the City of God: Rio de Janeiro and the Olympic Dream
author: Juliana Barbassa
note: Journalist Julianna Barbassa returns to her home city after a childhood spent largely abroad. Part memoir, part expose, part investigative journalism, it covers the national and city politicians' ill-considered efforts to prepare Rio to host the 2014 World Cup and 2016 Olympics. Essential Brasil read.

title: Demon Copperhead (
author: Barbara Kingsolver
note: My friend who pastors at Doxology Church mentioned this book during a sermon about timeless stories, discussing it in the context of Dickens' David Copperfield as a story that transcends time periods and locales. This Pulitzer prize winning tale is a HARD read that lays bare the realities of the foster system, rural America, the opioid crisis, and growing up as a teen today. The narrated version is INCREDIBLE to listen to--highly recommend.

title: Greenlights
author: Matthew McConaughey
note: Don't read this, I repeat, don't read this--LISTEN TO IT. The Audible version is narrated by author Matthew McConaughey and he's phenomenal: vulnerable, funny, self-deprecating, raw, and at times outrageous.

title: How to Know a Person: The Art of Seeing Others Deeply and Being Seen
author: David Brooks
summary: A phenomenal and important read. I listened to it on Audible and about 1/3 of the way through I found it so incredible that I ordered a physical copy so I could reread it and make extensive notes. Author David Brooks offers a framework by which one could be not a better leader but a more caring and empathetic friend and person in general. I particularly enjoyed some of the questions he uses to better understand where people come from. One example: In your family what was one thing you could never do?
quotes: In your family what was one thing you could never do?

title: How to Stop Time
author: Matt Haig
note: By the author of Midnight Library. Matt Haig is a gifted storyteller, and I loved his tale of a secret society of quasi-immortals who, through a genetic anomaly, age only 1 year for every 14 years on earth.

title: The Collector of Leftover Souls: Field Notes on Brazil's Everyday Insurrections.
author: Eliane Brum
note: This book is a collection of essays by reporter Eliane Brum in which she sheds light on different facets of Brazilian society normally buried in darkness: indigenous peoples, social minorities, the people who live on the margins of society, far from the centers of power. Essential Brasil read.

title: The Extraordinary Mountain Creatures of North America.
author: Betty Kruse (my daughter)
note: My 12 year old daughter Betty's first (self) published novel.

title: The Invisibles
author: Ed Siegle
note: Came across this book on a colleague's bookshelf and he kindly let me borrow it--I liked it so much I ordered a copy of my own. A British woman and a Brazilian man fall in love in the 70's and have a child, but he falls on hard times and ends up jailed under the military dictatorship.

title: Why I am Still Surprised by the Power of the Spirit: Discovering How God Speaks and Heals Today.
author: Jack Deere
note: Great read.

title: A Death in Brazil
author: Peter Robb
note: Published in 2004, in the second year of Lula's long-fought-for presidency, this book delves into the background of Brazil's first popularly elected president Fernando Collor de Mello and the murder of his campaign treasurer. In telling the story of Fernando and PC's rise, the author contrasts it with current President Lula's populist ascension and ambitiously lays it all across a selected violent history of Brazil.
quote: Brazilians expected little of their leaders and even less of the mob in Congress.

title: A Moveable Feast
author: Ernest Hemingway
note: Hemingway's novel hit different this time around, reading it at the midpoint of my life, having walked the same Parisian streets and cafes and lived nearly a decade of my own life overseas.

title: A Place on Earth
author: Wendell Berry
note: Berry's writing is somehow comforting--that's a strange thing to say about a novel--it's the rare book that is more observation than narrative. Ostensibly the novel is about a town during World War II. But the novel is actually about the land, the environment and seasons, the floods and droughts, the earth and woods--somehow Berry has written a story where the people are the scenery.

title: All the Light We Cannot See
author: Anthony Doerr
note: The main character, a blind teenager named Marie-Laure, questions as the Nazi Paris invasion uproots her comfortable and predictable life. What unfolds is a story that seesaws between shocking and inspiring, horrifying and redemptive and which stands as a stark reminder of what a world at war looks like at the most visceral level. As I tore through this novel in 3 quick days, I lamented that I'd let it waste away for so long on our bookshelf.
quote: Who knew love could kill you?

title: Andy Catlett: Early Travels
author: Wendell Berry
note: My third Wendell Berry novel this year. This was a quick, light read covering the childhood experiences of a young 9-year-old Andy Catlett as he spends time on his grandparents' farm and learns about his family's deep connection to the land.

title: Barbarians at the Gate
author: Bryan Burrough & John Helyar
note: I accidentally got the abridged version instead of the unabridged, which ended up being a good call, as this book was just okay for me--not being super into business and deal-making, I shouldn't have been surprised.

title: Caste: The Origin of our Discontent
author: Isabel Wilkerson
note: The book is at its most powerful in her research on how the Nazi party studied the caste system in the United States and sought to emulate it in its systemic subjugation and extermination of the Jewish population.

title: Cutting for Stone
author: Abraham Verghese
summary: Verghese's sweeping novel of twin brothers born in an Ethiopian mission hospital and separated by circumstance and destiny. One of the great medical novels ever written, and one of the great African novels. Verghese writes about the body, about surgery, about Ethiopia with a precision and love that is stunning.

title: East of Eden
author: John Steinbeck
note: While his earlier Grapes of Wrath may have earned him a Pulitzer, East of Eden is widely acknowledged as a better and more mature novel, and I was surprised at how readable it was 70 years after its original publication.
quote: I always found in myself a dread of West and a love of East.

title: Elizabeth Ritchie and the Kingdom of Whatnots
author: Betty Kruse
note: A young girl gets called into another world in a plot to assassinate an evil king--chaos ensues (My 12 year old daughter Betty's next book which she'll self-publish).

title: Fresh Water for Flowers
author: Valérie Perrin
note: Was listening to this novel on Libby, but I think we gave up. Not our cup of fresh water tea.

title: Gabriela, Clove and Cinnamon
author: Jorge Amado
note: Author Jorge Amado is one of the best known Brazilian writers so I thought it was high time that I read one of his novels. Gabriela is a story of a 1920's Bahian cacao town struggling against modernity whose entire arc is upended by a poor and beautiful young woman from the northeast. For this reader, I likely won't revisit Amado but it was a good data point on Brazilian literature.

title: Hannah Coulter
author: Wendell Berry
summary: Berry's gentle, devastating novel about a Kentucky farm woman looking back on her life — two husbands, her children, the land, the community of Port William. It is a novel about what endures when everything else falls away. Reading Berry always feels like being told the truth about something you've always known but never been able to say.

title: How to Stay Married
author: Harrison Scott Key
note: This book's power comes from humorist author Harrison Scott Key's fearlessness in revealing the most raw, intimate emotions surrounding his wife's infidelity.

title: James
author: Percival Everett
summary: Pulitzer Prize winner. Everett reimagines Huckleberry Finn from Jim's perspective, and in doing so transforms one of American literature's most troubling relationships into something revelatory. One of the most important American novels in years.

title: Leaf by Niggle
author: J.R.R. Tolkien
note: A good friend gave me a copy of this J.R.R. Tolkien short story that is ostensibly about an artist and his painting of a tree but which of course is about much more. Tolkien wrote it as he struggled with his own anxiety over whether he'd ever be able to finish the Silmarillion.

title: May the Wolf Die
author: Elizabeth Heidler
note: Named one of the New York Times best crime novels of 2024, this debut from scientist/researcher/author Elizabeth Heidler nails all the gritty Naples details down to the trash-strewn highways that contrast with the breathtaking coastal waters.

title: Nathan Coulter
author: Wendell Berry
note: The first novel from Wendell Berry's Port Williams series. I regret not having read anything from Berry's considerable compendium until this year--I recall rolling my eyes years ago when my mother raved about his Memory of Old Jack, but a sermon illustration on being "pure in heart" got me hooked.

title: Our Missing Hearts
author: Celeste Ng
note: Celeste Ng has penned a beautiful and thought-provoking dystopian story about a United States where blind allegiance to country has stamped out any criticism or dissent, no matter how constructive. Children are taken, the "missing hearts" of the title, from any families not deemed appropriately loyal to the Pact.

title: Praying Like Monks, Living Like Fools
author: Tyler Staton
note: Author and pastor Tyler Staton lays out a persuasive call for the church broadly, and the believer personally, to return to the prayer practices of the early church and Jesus.
quote: Prayer is a journey that starts in need and ends in relationship.

title: Reasons to Stay Alive
author: Matt Haig
note: I've enjoyed reading Matt Haig's novels over the last several years, and when I saw he had written a very raw memoir I was eager to read it. That it turned out to also be a "how to be a friend to someone suffering from depression" guide made it an especially powerful and necessary read.

title: Right-Hand Shore
author: Christopher Tilghman
note: Reading Tilghman's writing is effortless, which means he's both incredibly talented and works hard at it. His 2012 tale, one of four in a series I found out later, of a family's doomed farm on the Eastern shore stretches across generations from the Civil War through Reconstruction.

title: Rumors of Another World
author: Philip Yancey
note: This is a weighty book, not one you can breeze through quickly, but rather one that calls the reader towards a meditative examination of how the world we live in can't be fully natural or supernatural--it's not an either/or choice.

title: Someone Like Us
author: Dinaw Mengestu
note: Far less sweeping than his previous novels, this still builds out Mengestu's tapestry of the immigrant experience in America by focusing on an Ethiopian father and son's evolving relationship.

title: Street Girls: Hope on the Streets of Brazil
author: Matt Roper
note: This is British author/journalist Matt Roper's first of four books highlighting the child-trafficking and child-prostitution problem in Brazil.

title: The Covenant of Water
author: Abraham Verghese
note: Spanning nearly a century (1900-1977), Covenant takes place in southwestern India in the Kerala region and focuses on a Malayali Christian family and a mysterious drowning affliction that affects someone in each generation.
quote: This is the covenant of water: that they're all linked inescapably by their acts of commission and omission, and no one stands alone... all is one.

title: The Life Impossible
author: Matt Haig
note: In the vein of Gabriel Garcia Marquez, Matt Haig's latest tale is a magical realist amble into the intersection of mathematics, environmentalism, science, philosophy, extraterrestrial life, loss, and grief.

title: The Ruthless Elimination of Hurry
author: Tim Comer
summary: Life-changing. Comer's core argument is that hurry is the enemy of the spiritual life — and that the pace of modern life is fundamentally incompatible with following Jesus. The solution is not a productivity hack but a wholesale reorientation of how you move through time. This led me to read Practicing the Way the following year.

title: When the Cranes Fly South
author: Lisa Ridzen
note: The book very well could have been called "Things Unspoken," given the main character's intense interior monologue of emotions, outcries and thoughts that he largely keeps locked up throughout the entire novel.

title: You Shall Know Our Velocity
author: Dave Eggers
note: Do NOT recommend. It very mysteriously appeared on our bookshelf when I was organizing and I thought it must be some type of sign--it was not.

title: A Day in the Life of Abed Salama: Anatomy of a Jerusalem Tragedy
author: Nathan Thrall
note: Pulitzer Prize Winner. Read with my oldest daughter. Jewish-American author Nathan Thrall first wrote this story as a long form journalistic piece for the New York Review of Books, and I can't imagine this made him any friends within the Israeli government.

title: A Marriage at Sea
author: Sophie Elmhirst
note: In 1973 a British couple sailing to New Zealand, with no radios or transmitters, collided with a whale. Their boat sank and yet somehow they survived, lost at sea, with only a life raft and dinghy for 117 days.

title: America Fantastica
author: Tim O'Brien
note: In America Fantastica O'Brien widens his scope and tackles a host of characters--all seemingly without a moral compass.

title: Before the Night Comes
author: Matt Roper
note: I first learned about Matt Roper's work through a friend who is setting up an American arm of Meninadanca to help fundraise and spread awareness about the child prostitution and trafficking problem along Brazil's 2800 mile long BR-116 highway.

title: Bird by Bird: Some Instruction on Writing and Life
author: Anne Lamott
note: Found this book sitting on my book shelf, what a welcome surprise! This was one of my pre-bedtime reads and I finished it off in a week--Anne Lamott can write!
quote: Nothing is as important as a likable narrator. Nothing holds a story together better.

title: Hamnet
author: Maggie O'Farrell
summary: First thing to set straight: during Shakespeare's time, the names 'Hamnet' and 'Hamlet' were used interchangeably. Author Maggie O'Farrell can write. She crafts a fictional-ish narrative that connects Shakespeare's 11-year-old son Hamnet's death to his writing of his famous play. She stacks that narrative with layers of lush writing, conversations, and descriptions that transport you to the middle of that bubonic time period on the macro level while at the same time embedding you inside of unbearable loss. I won't spoil the ending but I will say that the movie's ending is far more satisfying than the book. There's a moment there in the movie as Hamnet's mother watches her husband's play that hangs on the screen as your heart breaks. The power of Shakespeare lives on 426 years later!

title: Jayber Crow
author: Wendell Berry
note: Jayber Crow is the name of the main character and covers the arc of his life from a young orphaned boy to an aging, partially retired town barber looking back on his life. His life takes a wandering route from the orphanage barber apprentice to seminary dropout to prison chain gang to eventual Port Williams barber, gravedigger, and church custodian.

title: Letters by a Modern Mystic: Excerpts from Letters Written at Dansalan, Lake Lanao, Philippine Islands, to His Father
author: Frank C. Laubach
note: Currently reading as morning devotional.

title: My Life as a Student and Teacher at Woodberry Forest School: A Memoir
author: Paul Huber
note: Written by renowned professor and administrator Paul Huber. This reads less as biography and more as loose recollection and scattered history of the prestigious all-boys boarding school founded in 1889, of which I am a proud 1997 grad.

title: New Boy
author: Murphy Evans
note: Written by 1979 Woodberry Forest School Senior Prefect Murphy Evans, the novel takes place at the fictional "Randolph School" and focuses on the lives of the adolescent boys at an all-boys boarding school.

title: Practicing the Way: Be with Jesus.  Become Like Him. Do as He Did.
author: Tim Comer
summary: Last year we read Comer's 'Ruthless Elimination of Hurry' and it was life changing. This book is for those who read Ruthless and ask themselves: so how do we actually implement an unhurried and contemplative life? Practicing the Way lays out a path that is a call first and foremost to eat Jesus' dust — this refers to the Jewish practice of following and imitating a rabbi so closely that you literally would be walking in his dust. It's noteworthy that the term Christian appears only 3 times in the New Testament, while the word for 'Apprentice' (mathetes) appears 269 times.
quotes: The term Christian appears only 3 times in the New Testament, while the word for Apprentice (mathetes) appears 269 times.

title: The Lemon Tree: An Arab, a Jew, and the Heart of the Middle East
author: Sandy Tolan
note: National Book Critics Circle Award Finalist. This is book 2 of 3 in an ongoing conversation with my oldest daughter Macee on the Israel-Palestine conflict.
quote: Our enemy... is the only partner we have.

title: The Playground
author: Richard Powers
note: After reading Powers' incredible The Overstory my hopes were sky high for his 2024 globe-spanning novel on the coral reefs of the Pacific. I was ready to have my mind blown and expanded on coral reefs in the same way he had done with trees.

title: Theo of Golden
author: Allen Levi
summary: There's not much allegorical fiction being written these days. Theo of Golden makes me want a lot more of it — at least more of it written by Allan Levi. Published in 2023 but somehow I only heard of the novel a month ago from a pastor friend named Steve. The story is about an octogenarian named Theo who mysteriously arrives in the town of Golden and proceeds to — incredibly — get to know perfect strangers! He uses the gift of hand drawn portraits as the mechanism in this quest and along the way offers a winsome example of kindness in action.

title: Tomcat in Love
author: Tim O'Brien
note: Tim O'Brien can write. His sentence and dialogue and paragraph flow masterfully--he's a quick read, there's no Faulkner-esque chewing of paragraphs required to digest. But after every book I read, I consider the fundamental reader's question: would I recommend it? After I finished Tomcat and jotted down some key quotes that captured some superb writing, I tossed it in the trash.
quote: Nothing is as important as a likable narrator. Nothing holds a story together better.

title: A Personal Matter
author: Kenzaburō Ōe
note: Unless you enjoy spending a whole book despising the main (and pretty much the only) character--don't waste your time on it. Oe's redemptive efforts in the last three pages are a copout and ring blaringly hollow.

title: Arrow of God
author: Chinua Achebe
note: I was first struck by how funny this novel was. I guffawed several times while reading it. It takes a remarkable writer to do this with humor, especially across cultures.
